title: SEGMENT 5 — Building colors by tweaking the chromophore

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — Building colors by tweaking the chromophore
Now let me show you how you change the color, because once you have the barrel-and-chromophore picture, the color engineering is going to feel almost inevitable.
Here's the principle.
The color of light the chromophore absorbs and emits depends on two things.
First, the chemical structure of the chromophore itself — its electronic skeleton, the conjugated double bonds.

Second, the chemical environment surrounding the chromophore inside the barrel — the nearby amino acids whose electrostatic and steric properties nudge the chromophore's energy levels one way or another.
Change either of those, and you change the color.
The analogy I want you to hold is tinting.
Imagine the chromophore is a light bulb, and the amino acids that touch it from the inside of the barrel are a set of colored filters and surface coatings pressed up against the bulb.
Swap out a filter, and the color shifts.
Coat the bulb with a different material, and the color shifts.
The bulb itself can also be swapped — replace the tyrosine in the center with a histidine or a tryptophan, and you've changed the actual emitting material, which shifts the color even more.
Let me walk you through the canonical color variants of the GFP family, because these are the proteins you'll actually encounter in modern papers.
Start with wild-type GFP, straight from the jellyfish.
As I mentioned, it had a few problems — two awkward absorption peaks instead of one clean one, modest brightness, slow folding at 37 degrees Celsius, which is mammalian body temperature.
The jellyfish lives in cold water, so the protein evolved to fold at ocean temperatures, not warm-blooded ones.
The first big improvement was a single point mutation discovered in 1995: S65T — swap the serine at position 65 for a threonine.
This single change cleaned up the absorption spectrum to a single nice peak.
Combined with another mutation — F64L, phenylalanine to leucine — which fixed the folding problem at body temperature, you got the variant called EGFP, "enhanced green fluorescent protein.

" EGFP absorbs cleanly at 488 nanometers — which conveniently happened to be the standard wavelength of the argon laser already sitting on every confocal microscope in the world — and emits at 507 nanometers.
It folds well at 37 degrees.
It's about thirty-five times brighter than wild-type GFP.
EGFP became, and remains, the default green fluorescent protein in most labs.
When someone says "GFP" today, they almost always mean EGFP.
Now the other colors.
Stay with me, because there's a pattern, and the pattern is the structural lesson.
EBFP — enhanced blue fluorescent protein.
To shift the emission to blue, you swap out the tyrosine at the center for a histidine.

The histidine has a smaller, differently-shaped ring than tyrosine, and it gives a different electronic structure to the chromophore, which shifts the emission from green to blue, around 440 nanometers.
Early blue variants were dim and bleached fast, but later versions like EBFP2 are much better.
ECFP — enhanced cyan fluorescent protein, emitting in the cyan, around 475 nanometers, between blue and green.
The mutation here is also at position 66, but now to tryptophan.
The bulky indole ring of tryptophan in the center of the barrel gives the cyan color.
CFP turns out to be important because it pairs beautifully with YFP for a technique called FRET, which we'll cover in Segment 10.
EYFP — enhanced yellow fluorescent protein.
To shift in the other direction, toward yellow, you keep the original tyrosine in the middle but tweak the environment.
Specifically, a mutation called T203Y places an aromatic ring right next to the chromophore.
Two aromatic rings stacked next to each other lower the chromophore's excited-state energy, which red-shifts the emission into the yellow, around 527 nanometers.
There are improved YFP variants — Citrine, Venus, mVenus — that fold faster and are less sensitive to small changes in pH and chloride.
So look at the logic.

To make significant color shifts, the most powerful lever is to change the amino acid at position 66, the central one — to histidine for blue, to tryptophan for cyan, leave it as tyrosine for green or yellow.
Position 66 is, in a very literal sense, the color knob of the protein.
The surrounding amino acids provide fine adjustments — tweaking brightness, folding speed, the exact wavelength.
And here's the payoff.
Once you have a palette of spectrally distinct variants — blue, cyan, green, yellow — you can label multiple proteins in the same cell with different colors.
You can dye the mitochondria one color and the actin cytoskeleton another and the nucleus a third, and watch all three simultaneously in a single living cell.
You can use two compatible colors to ask whether two proteins are interacting, via the FRET trick we'll cover later.
Multi-color imaging is where most of the experimental power lives, and you can't do it without a palette.
The whole point of Tsien's engineering campaign was to build that palette.
But there was still a gap.
None of these variants emitted in the red.
The longest-wavelength Aequorea-derived variant, YFP, tops out around 530 nanometers — that's yellow, not red.
For deeper tissue imaging, for cleaner separation from green, for more channels, the field needed red.
And the red wasn't going to come from jellyfish.
It was going to come from coral.
